Geography of Rights

A state-level human rights policy index · 50 states + DC · April 2026
DSS Project
Start here

What does it look like to live under the law in your state?

Every U.S. state writes its own rules for some of the most important parts of our lives — who can get healthcare, who can vote, what workers are paid, and how the criminal system treats people. This project scores all 50 states and DC on five of those rights, then combines them into one 0–100 score showing how strong protections are overall. Higher means stronger rights protections.

1Click any state on the map — or pick from the dropdown.
2See its score in each of the five rights areas.
3Open any row to learn what the score means and why it matters.

Compare states side-by-side

Step 1. Pick a rights area to compare. Step 2. Click any states you want to stack up. The chart rebuilds as you go — and you can see how each state's sub-indicators roll up into the dimension score.

What goes with what

The darker the square, the more the two things move together across states. States tend to protect rights in bundles — it's rare to see a state that's very strong on one right and very weak on another.

Rights areas ↔ Rights areas

State characteristics ↔ Rights scores

The biggest single predictor

Of all the state characteristics we tested — income, urbanization, race, unemployment — the strongest single predictor of overall rights score is how the state voted in the 2024 presidential election.

Research methodology

This tab is for readers who want the technical detail — specifications, regression tables, weighting choices. The rest of the site is written for general audiences.

How the index is built

Each of the five dimensions contains 5–6 sub-indicators, coded so that higher values always mean stronger rights protections. All sub-indicators are min-max normalized to 0–100. Each dimension score is the unweighted mean of its normalized sub-indicators. The composite is the unweighted mean of the five dimension scores (20% each).

Equal weighting vs. PCA weighting

A principal component analysis on the five standardized dimension scores gives PC1 explaining 81.7% of variance with all positive loadings — meaning the five dimensions essentially measure one underlying "rights regime" axis. The Spearman rank correlation between the equal-weight composite and a PCA-weighted composite is 0.9993. Equal weights are retained for interpretability.

OLS regression — how much do state predictors explain?

Four nested specifications regress composite score on demographic/political predictors, using N = 51:

Preferred spec: M2 Political lean + income + urbanization. Full spec M4 is over-parameterized given N=51 and suffers from multicollinearity among race/income variables.

M2 — Standardized β (95% CI)

Caveats to read carefully

  • Dimensions correlate positively. All ten off-diagonal dimension pairs have Pearson r in [0.64, 0.90]. This reflects real-world policy bundling across U.S. states, not a flaw in the index. Claims about trade-offs between rights areas should always reference individual dimension scores, not the composite.
  • Cross-sectional snapshot. Policy is coded as of April 2026. The Brennan Center's "net voting laws 2020–2025" sub-indicator is the only one that captures trajectory rather than current state.
  • No enforcement data. This is a policy index, not an outcome measure. It does not capture whether rights on the books are actually honored — that requires different data (e.g., ballot rejection rates, abortion referral availability, housing discrimination complaints).
  • Reverse-coded indicators (voter ID strictness, religious exemptions, right-to-work) are flagged throughout so readers can see when a policy was flipped to fit the "higher = stronger rights" direction.

Data sources

  • Reproductive Rights: Guttmacher Institute, Center for Reproductive Rights
  • LGBTQ+ Protections: Movement Advancement Project Equality Maps, Williams Institute
  • Voting Access: Brennan Center for Justice, NCSL, Sentencing Project
  • Labor Rights: Oxfam Best States to Work Index, U.S. DOL, A Better Balance
  • Criminal Justice: Death Penalty Information Center, Prison Policy Initiative, Sentencing Project
  • Predictors: Census ACS 5-year, MIT Election Data & Science Lab, BLS, KFF, NCSL
